title: AP World History: Modern DBQ Practice Exam 08

questions.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which women’s claims to political participation changed from c. 1900 to 1975.
Document 1
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Petition by members of a women’s suffrage association to the New Zealand Parliament, 1893, Wellington.
Source Type: Political petition.
“Women obey the laws, pay taxes, raise children, teach in schools, and contribute to the moral welfare of the colony. A government that claims to represent the people cannot continue to exclude half of the adult population from the vote. We ask not for privilege but for equal citizenship.”
Sourcing cues: Organized suffrage activists addressing lawmakers; frames voting rights as citizenship, morality, and representation.
Document 2
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Speech by Huda al-Masri, an Egyptian women’s education advocate, at a public lecture, 1924, Cairo. This source is original but modeled on early twentieth-century Middle Eastern reform arguments.
Source Type: Public speech.
“A nation cannot demand independence from foreign rule while keeping its daughters dependent within the home. Education does not destroy family honor; ignorance does. If women are to raise citizens, manage charities, write, teach, and serve the nation, they must be recognized as participants in public life.”
Sourcing cues: Urban educated reformer speaking after nationalist mobilization; links women’s education and public participation to national independence.
Document 3
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Description of a women’s labor poster issued by a textile union, 1936, Osaka.
Source Type: Described poster.
The poster shows a young woman standing between a loom and a ballot box. Behind her are crowded worker dormitories and a factory clock. The caption reads: “The Hands That Weave the Nation Deserve a Voice.” Smaller text calls for shorter hours, safe housing, and women’s participation in worker organizations.
Sourcing cues: Labor-union visual source; connects women’s wage labor, workplace reform, and political voice.
Document 4
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Statement by a member of the All-India Women’s Conference, 1946, Delhi.
Source Type: Organization statement.
“Women marched, taught, spun, picketed, and went to prison in the national struggle. Independence must not restore us to silence. The constitution must provide equal civil rights, education, and the vote, but laws alone will not change village custom, dowry burdens, or the belief that women belong only to the household.”
Sourcing cues: Women’s organization involved in nationalist and constitutional debates; distinguishes formal rights from social practice.
Document 5
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Plain-text table compiled by a United Nations research office, 1955.
Source Type: Data table.
Selected Women’s Voting Rights by Region, c. 1900–1955
Region / Country Example Approximate Pattern
New Zealand National voting rights for women before 1900
United Kingdom Expanded women’s suffrage after World War I
Turkey Women’s voting rights in the interwar period
India Universal adult suffrage adopted with independence
Mexico National women’s suffrage adopted in the 1950s
Several colonial territories Rights often delayed until late colonial or postcolonial reforms
Note on the table: Legal voting rights did not always produce equal representation in legislatures or equal influence within political parties.
Sourcing cues: International research perspective; useful for comparison across states but limited in measuring daily social equality.
Document 6
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Memoir excerpt by an Algerian woman who served as a courier during the war for independence, written in 1970, Algiers.
Source Type: Memoir excerpt.
“During the struggle, I carried messages under my clothes and crossed checkpoints where men were searched more closely. We were told that the revolution needed every daughter of the nation. After independence, some comrades thanked us, but others said politics was now for men with experience. I had risked prison for a country in which I still had to ask permission to speak.”
Sourcing cues: Retrospective account by a female participant in anti-colonial struggle; emphasizes both wartime participation and postwar exclusion.
Document 7
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Statement from a delegate at the International Women’s Year conference, 1975, Mexico City.
Source Type: Conference statement.
“Women’s political participation cannot be measured only by the vote. We must ask who owns land, who receives education, who controls wages, who sits in ministries, who speaks in unions, and who is protected by law. The experiences of women in newly independent countries, industrial states, and rural communities differ, but equality must include public power and daily life.”
Sourcing cues: International conference perspective; broadens political participation beyond suffrage and links gender equality to social and economic structures.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 7
Thesis / Claim — 1 point: Earns the point by making a historically defensible claim that evaluates the extent to which women’s claims to political participation changed from c. 1900 to 1975. A strong thesis should establish a line of reasoning, such as arguing that women’s claims expanded from suffrage and citizenship to broader demands for education, labor rights, constitutional equality, representation, and social power, while barriers in family, workplace, parties, and postcolonial states persisted.
Contextualization — 1 point: Earns the point by describing a broader historical context relevant to women’s political participation, such as industrial labor, suffrage movements, nationalism, World War I, World War II, decolonization, socialist and labor movements, international organizations, constitutional reform, or global feminist activism.
Evidence from the Documents — 2 points: Earns 1 point by accurately using the content of at least three documents to address the prompt. Earns 2 points by using at least six documents to support an argument about suffrage, education, labor activism, nationalism, constitutional rights, postcolonial participation, international conferences, or continuing social barriers.
Evidence Beyond the Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by using at least one specific piece of relevant outside evidence not found in the documents, such as British suffragettes, World War I home-front labor, Soviet policies on women, Chinese Communist marriage reform, women in the Mexican Revolution, Simone de Beauvoir, the UN Commission on the Status of Women, second-wave feminism, or women’s participation in Vietnam or Cuba.
Sourcing / Analysis of Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by explaining how or why the point of view, purpose, audience, or historical situation of at least three documents is relevant to the argument. Effective examples include explaining why suffrage petitioners appealed to lawmakers, why nationalist reformers linked women’s education to independence, why a labor poster connected factory work to political voice, why memoirs may highlight postwar disappointment, or why an international conference broadened the meaning of political participation.
Complexity — 1 point: Earns the point by demonstrating a more complex understanding of women’s political participation. This may include explaining both legal change and social continuity, comparing industrial, colonial, and postcolonial contexts, showing that nationalism opened opportunities but did not guarantee equality, or explaining that political participation expanded beyond voting to include labor, education, law, and representation.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong DBQ could argue that women’s claims to political participation changed significantly from c. 1900 to 1975 by moving beyond suffrage toward broader claims about labor, education, constitutional equality, nationalism, and representation. Documents 1 and 5 are useful for suffrage and legal rights. Documents 2 and 4 connect women’s participation to nationalism and education. Document 3 connects labor activism to political voice. Documents 6 and 7 show that wartime or revolutionary participation did not automatically produce equal postwar power and that international feminism broadened the meaning of political participation. Outside evidence could include British suffragettes, World War I labor, Chinese marriage reform, the UN Commission on the Status of Women, second-wave feminism, or women in revolutionary movements. Strong sourcing should compare petitions, speeches, labor propaganda, organizational statements, data tables, memoirs, and international conference rhetoric.
